title: Headache and Migraine — Shirahshula
content:
DISEASE: Shirahshula (Headache and Migraine). In Ayurveda, headaches are classified based on the dosha involved. Pitta-type headaches present as burning, throbbing pain often concentrated in the temples and behind the eyes, frequently triggered by heat, sun exposure, or spicy foods. Vata-type headaches manifest as a dull, shifting pain that worsens with anxiety, irregular meals, or lack of sleep. When both doshas are aggravated simultaneously, the condition becomes more intense and can escalate into a full migraine with nausea and light sensitivity.

DOSHA: Pitta and Vata doshas are aggravated. Pitta imbalance causes excess heat and inflammation in the blood and liver channels, which rises to the head. Vata imbalance disrupts the flow of prana vayu (the vital air governing the nervous system), causing erratic nerve signaling and pain.

SYMPTOMS: Throbbing or pulsating pain in the temples, forehead, or one side of the head. Sensitivity to light and sound. Nausea or vomiting in severe cases. Irritability and restlessness. Blurred vision or seeing auras before migraine onset. Pain worsens with exposure to sunlight, heat, or loud environments.

ROOT CAUSE: According to Ayurveda, the root cause is accumulation of ama (toxins) in the rasa and rakta dhatus (plasma and blood tissues), combined with aggravated Pitta causing inflammation and Vata causing irregular nerve impulses. Poor digestion, emotional stress, suppressed anger, irregular eating patterns, and excessive screen time are common contributing factors.

HERBS: 1) Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — Take 1 teaspoon of Brahmi powder mixed in warm water or milk twice daily. Brahmi calms Vata, nourishes the nervous system, and reduces the frequency of migraines. 2) Shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) — Prepare a decoction by boiling 5 grams of Shirisha bark in 200 ml water until reduced to 50 ml. Drink once daily to reduce Pitta-related inflammation. 3) Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Take 500 mg Jatamansi powder with honey before bed. It calms the mind, reduces anxiety-driven headaches, and promotes deep sleep.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Apply a paste of sandalwood powder mixed with rose water to the forehead and temples. Step 2: Place a cold cloth soaked in Brahmi-infused water on the forehead for 15 minutes. Step 3: Massage 2-3 drops of Brahmi oil or sesame oil on the scalp, focusing on the crown and temples, using gentle circular motions. Step 4: Drink a cup of warm milk with a pinch of turmeric and a half teaspoon of ghee before bed. Step 5: Practice slow, deep Anulom Vilom (alternate nostril breathing) for 10 minutes in a quiet, dark room.

DIET: Eat cooling foods such as cucumber, coconut water, coriander, fennel, and sweet fruits like pomegranate and grapes. Avoid spicy, sour, fermented, and fried foods. Avoid coffee, alcohol, and excessive salt. Eat meals at regular times — skipping meals aggravates Vata. Include ghee in your diet as it lubricates the nervous system and balances both Pitta and Vata.

YOGA: Practice Sheetali Pranayama (cooling breath). Sit comfortably, curl your tongue into a tube, inhale slowly through the curled tongue, close your mouth, and exhale through the nostrils. Repeat 15-20 rounds. This cools the body, reduces Pitta, and calms the nervous system. Also beneficial: Shavasana (corpse pose) for 10 minutes with eyes closed.

ESCALATE IF: Headache is sudden and extremely severe (thunderclap headache). Headache is accompanied by fever, stiff neck, confusion, or vision loss. Headache follows a head injury. Migraines occur more than 4 times per month despite home remedies. Headache is accompanied by weakness or numbness on one side of the body.

title: Acidity and Bloating — Amlapitta
content:
DISEASE: Amlapitta (Hyperacidity and Bloating). Amlapitta is one of the most common digestive disorders described in Ayurvedic texts. It occurs when aggravated Pitta dosha increases the production of acid (amla rasa) in the stomach, leading to a burning sensation, sour belching, and abdominal discomfort. When Vata is also involved, it causes bloating and distension of the abdomen due to trapped gas.

DOSHA: Pitta dosha is primarily aggravated. Pitta governs agni (digestive fire) and when it becomes excessive, the digestive fire burns too intensely, producing excess hydrochloric acid. This leads to inflammation of the stomach lining and esophagus. Kapha may also be disturbed, leading to a feeling of heaviness and nausea after meals.

SYMPTOMS: Burning sensation in the chest and upper abdomen. Sour or bitter belching. Bloating and abdominal distension after eating. Nausea, especially in the morning or after heavy meals. Loss of appetite or excessive hunger. Bad breath and a sour taste in the mouth. Occasional loose stools with a burning sensation.

ROOT CAUSE: The primary cause is excessive intake of Pitta-aggravating foods — spicy, sour, salty, oily, and fermented foods. Irregular eating habits, eating too fast, eating while stressed, excessive tea or coffee, alcohol consumption, and late-night meals all weaken the digestive system. Emotional factors like anger, frustration, and competitiveness also increase Pitta.

HERBS: 1) Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — Take 1 teaspoon of Yashtimadhu powder mixed in cold milk twice daily, 30 minutes before meals. It soothes the stomach lining and reduces acid secretion. 2) Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — Take 1 teaspoon of Shatavari powder in warm milk once daily. It cools the digestive tract and nourishes the stomach lining. 3) Avipattikar Churna — Take half a teaspoon with warm water after meals. This classical Ayurvedic formula directly addresses amlapitta by balancing stomach acid.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Drink a glass of cold milk with 1 teaspoon of ghee on an empty stomach in the morning. Step 2: Chew a small piece of fresh amla (Indian gooseberry) or take 1 teaspoon of amla powder with water before lunch. Step 3: Prepare jeera (cumin) water by boiling 1 teaspoon of cumin seeds in 500 ml water for 5 minutes, strain, and sip throughout the day. Step 4: After dinner, chew half a teaspoon of fennel seeds (saunf) to prevent bloating. Step 5: Avoid lying down for at least 2 hours after eating — take a gentle 10-minute walk instead.

DIET: Favor cooling, sweet, and bitter foods — rice, wheat, moong dal, bottle gourd, ash gourd, cucumber, and sweet fruits like banana and melon. Drink coconut water daily. Avoid tomatoes, citrus fruits, vinegar, pickles, onion, garlic, chilies, fried foods, and caffeinated drinks. Eat smaller, more frequent meals. Always eat in a calm environment without distractions.

YOGA: Practice Vajrasana (Thunderbolt Pose) for 5-10 minutes immediately after meals. Sit on your heels with knees together, spine straight, and palms resting on your thighs. This pose directs blood flow to the digestive organs and significantly improves digestion and reduces bloating. Also practice Pawanmuktasana (Wind-Relieving Pose) to release trapped gas.

ESCALATE IF: You experience severe abdominal pain that does not respond to antacids. There is blood in vomit or black-colored stools. Difficulty swallowing or persistent vomiting. Unintended weight loss along with digestive issues. Symptoms persist for more than 2 weeks despite dietary changes and home remedies.

title: Fever and Cold — Jwara and Pratishyaya
content:
DISEASE: Jwara (Fever) and Pratishyaya (Common Cold). Jwara is described in the Charaka Samhita as the king of diseases, affecting body, mind, and senses simultaneously. When Kapha dosha is aggravated, it produces excess mucus, congestion, and heaviness. When Vata dosha is disturbed, it causes chills, body aches, and irregular temperature regulation. The combination results in the classic cold and fever presentation.

DOSHA: Kapha and Vata doshas are aggravated. Kapha accumulation blocks the channels (srotas) of rasa dhatu, trapping heat inside and causing fever. Excess Kapha also produces mucus congestion in the respiratory tract. Vata imbalance causes chills, body pain, and restlessness. The digestive fire (agni) becomes weakened, producing ama (toxins) that circulate through the body.

SYMPTOMS: Elevated body temperature with alternating chills and sweating. Runny or blocked nose with thick, whitish-yellow mucus. Body aches and heaviness in the limbs. Sore throat and mild cough. Loss of appetite and a heavy feeling in the stomach. Fatigue and desire to sleep. Headache with heaviness, especially in the forehead and sinus areas.

ROOT CAUSE: Ayurveda attributes fever and cold to weakened agni (digestive fire) that allows ama (metabolic toxins) to accumulate. Exposure to cold winds, sudden weather changes, consuming cold or heavy foods during Kapha season, suppression of natural urges, and lowered immunity from stress or sleep deprivation are key triggers. The ama blocks the body's channels, disrupting normal heat regulation.

HERBS: 1) Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum / Holy Basil) — Boil 10-12 fresh tulsi leaves in 2 cups of water with a small piece of ginger and 2 black peppercorns until reduced to 1 cup. Add honey after cooling slightly. Drink twice daily. Tulsi is a powerful immunomodulator and respiratory cleanser. 2) Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — Take 1 teaspoon of Guduchi powder or 2 Guduchi tablets twice daily with warm water. It is called 'Amritavalli' (the root of immortality) and is the premier Ayurvedic immunomodulator. 3) Sitopaladi Churna — Take half a teaspoon mixed with honey three times daily. This classical formula clears respiratory congestion and reduces fever.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Prepare a Kashayam (herbal decoction) by boiling 1 teaspoon each of dry ginger powder, turmeric, and tulsi leaves in 3 cups of water until reduced to 1 cup. Add a teaspoon of honey after cooling to drinkable temperature. Drink this twice daily. Step 2: Do steam inhalation with 3-4 drops of eucalyptus oil in hot water for 10 minutes, covering your head with a towel. Step 3: Apply a thin paste of ginger juice and honey on the forehead to relieve sinus pressure. Step 4: Eat only light, warm, easily digestible foods — khichdi (rice and moong dal cooked together with turmeric) is ideal. Step 5: Rest in a warm room and avoid cold drafts, cold water, and heavy foods.

DIET: Eat warm, light, and easily digestible foods only. Khichdi with ghee, clear vegetable soups, warm rice kanji (rice gruel), and steamed vegetables are ideal. Drink warm water throughout the day — never cold water. Add black pepper, ginger, turmeric, and cumin to your cooking. Avoid dairy products (they increase mucus), fried foods, sweets, bananas, and cold beverages. Fast or eat very lightly until the fever breaks, as the body needs to divert energy from digestion to fighting the infection.

YOGA: Practice Bhastrika Pranayama (Bellows Breath) gently — sit upright, take rapid forceful breaths through both nostrils for 10 rounds, then rest. This generates internal heat, clears nasal passages, and stimulates the immune system. Only practice when fever is mild (below 100°F). If fever is high, practice gentle Anulom Vilom (alternate nostril breathing) instead, which balances Vata without generating excess heat.

ESCALATE IF: Fever exceeds 103°F (39.5°C) or persists for more than 3 days. Fever is accompanied by severe headache and stiff neck. Difficulty breathing or persistent chest pain develops. Rash appears along with the fever. There is confusion, disorientation, or seizures. Fever occurs in infants, elderly, or immunocompromised individuals.

title: Fatigue and Low Energy — Daurbalya
content:
DISEASE: Daurbalya (Chronic Fatigue and Low Energy). In Ayurveda, persistent fatigue indicates a fundamental depletion of ojas — the vital essence that sustains immunity, vitality, and mental clarity. When Vata dosha is aggravated, it depletes the body's energy reserves, dries out the tissues, and disrupts the natural rhythms of sleep and activity. This leads to a cycle of exhaustion that rest alone cannot resolve.

DOSHA: Vata dosha is primarily aggravated. Vata governs all movement in the body including nerve impulses, circulation, and elimination. When Vata becomes excessive due to overwork, irregular routines, or mental stress, it depletes ojas (vital energy) and weakens agni (digestive fire). Weakened agni means nutrients from food are not properly absorbed, leading to malnourishment at the tissue level despite eating adequately. This creates a vicious cycle of depletion.

SYMPTOMS: Persistent tiredness that does not improve with sleep. Feeling physically drained and heavy-limbed. Mental fog, poor concentration, and forgetfulness. Low motivation and apathy. Frequent yawning and desire to lie down. Dry skin and dull complexion. Irregular appetite — sometimes ravenous, sometimes no appetite at all. Muscle weakness and joint stiffness, especially in the morning.

ROOT CAUSE: Ayurveda identifies the root cause as Vata aggravation through excessive physical or mental activity without adequate rest and nourishment. Staying up late, skipping meals, consuming dry and cold foods, excessive travel, chronic worry, and overuse of stimulants (caffeine, sugar) all deplete ojas. The underlying mechanism is that aggravated Vata increases the catabolic (breaking-down) processes faster than the body can rebuild, leading to tissue depletion and exhaustion.

HERBS: 1) Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Take 1 teaspoon of Ashwagandha powder (churna) in warm milk with a pinch of cardamom and half a teaspoon of ghee, twice daily — morning and before bed. Ashwagandha is the premier Rasayana (rejuvenating) herb for Vata, rebuilding ojas and strengthening the nervous system. 2) Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — Take 1 teaspoon in warm milk once daily. It nourishes all seven dhatus (body tissues) and is especially beneficial for restoring depleted energy. 3) Amalaki (Emblica officinalis / Indian Gooseberry) — Take 1 teaspoon of Amalaki powder with honey in the morning. It is the richest natural source of Vitamin C and a powerful Rasayana that rejuvenates all tissues.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Begin each morning with a self-massage (Abhyanga) using warm sesame oil. Massage the entire body for 10-15 minutes, paying special attention to the feet, scalp, and lower back. This calms Vata, nourishes the tissues, and improves circulation. Step 2: Prepare an energy-building tonic: warm 1 cup of milk with 1 teaspoon Ashwagandha powder, half teaspoon ghee, a pinch of saffron, and a pinch of nutmeg. Drink this every night before bed. Step 3: Eat dates soaked overnight in ghee (2-3 dates) as a mid-morning snack — this is a classical Vata-pacifying energy food. Step 4: Take a 15-20 minute afternoon rest (not full sleep) to allow the body to recharge. Step 5: Go to bed by 10 PM and wake by 6 AM to align with the body's natural circadian rhythm.

DIET: Eat warm, moist, nourishing foods cooked with healthy fats — ghee, sesame oil, and coconut oil. Favor sweet, sour, and salty tastes which pacify Vata. Include warm soups, stews, whole grains like rice and wheat, root vegetables like sweet potato and beets, nuts (almonds, walnuts), and warm spiced milk. Eat at regular times — breakfast by 8 AM, lunch by noon (the largest meal), dinner by 7 PM (light). Avoid raw salads, cold smoothies, dry crackers, popcorn, and excessive caffeine which all aggravate Vata.

YOGA: Practice Yoga Nidra (Yogic Sleep) for 20-30 minutes daily. Lie in Shavasana, close your eyes, and follow a guided body scan, progressively relaxing each body part from toes to crown. One session of Yoga Nidra provides the equivalent rest of several hours of sleep. This is the most effective yoga practice for rebuilding ojas and resolving deep fatigue without requiring physical exertion.

ESCALATE IF: Fatigue persists for more than 4 weeks despite lifestyle changes. There is unexplained weight loss along with fatigue. Fatigue is accompanied by persistent low-grade fever. There is excessive thirst and frequent urination (may indicate diabetes). You experience shortness of breath or heart palpitations with minimal exertion. There are signs of depression — persistent sadness, loss of interest, or thoughts of self-harm.

title: Sore Throat and Cough — Kantharoga and Kasa
content:
DISEASE: Kantharoga (Sore Throat) and Kasa (Cough). In Ayurveda, sore throat and cough are primarily disorders of the Pranavaha Srotas (respiratory channels). When Kapha dosha is aggravated, it produces excess mucus that accumulates in the throat and chest, causing congestion, a feeling of heaviness, and a productive cough. The throat becomes inflamed as the body tries to expel the excess Kapha through coughing and throat irritation.

DOSHA: Kapha dosha is primarily aggravated. Excess Kapha increases the production of mucus (shleshma) in the respiratory tract, coating the throat and airways. This creates an ideal environment for infection and inflammation. The body responds by triggering the cough reflex to expel the accumulated mucus. In some cases, Vata may also be involved, causing a dry, tickling cough without mucus production.

SYMPTOMS: Pain and scratchiness in the throat, especially when swallowing. Productive cough with thick, white or yellowish mucus. Feeling of a lump in the throat. Hoarseness or loss of voice. Swollen or tender lymph nodes in the neck. Mild fever and body heaviness. Post-nasal drip causing throat irritation, especially when lying down. Chest congestion and a feeling of heaviness in the upper chest.

ROOT CAUSE: Kapha accumulates in the respiratory channels due to consumption of cold, heavy, and sweet foods, especially dairy products, ice cream, cold beverages, and refined sugars. Exposure to cold and damp weather, lack of physical activity, sleeping during the day, and suppressing the natural urge to cough or sneeze all contribute. The accumulated Kapha weakens local immunity in the throat, making it susceptible to infection.

HERBS: 1) Yashtimadhu (Licorice root) — Chew on a small piece of licorice root throughout the day, or take 1 teaspoon of Yashtimadhu powder mixed in warm water with honey three times daily. It soothes the inflamed throat, reduces cough, and has natural antibacterial properties. 2) Tulsi (Holy Basil) — Chew 5-6 fresh tulsi leaves twice daily or drink tulsi tea made from boiling 10 leaves in water. Tulsi has powerful antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that clear respiratory infections. 3) Trikatu Churna (a combination of dry ginger, black pepper, and long pepper) — Take a quarter teaspoon with honey twice daily. This powerfully reduces Kapha, clears congestion, and reignites the digestive fire.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Prepare a gargle solution by adding half a teaspoon of turmeric powder and half a teaspoon of rock salt to a glass of warm water. Gargle for 30 seconds and spit. Repeat 3-4 times daily. Step 2: Make a honey-ginger paste by mixing 1 teaspoon of freshly grated ginger juice with 1 teaspoon of raw honey. Take this mixture slowly, letting it coat the throat, three times daily. Step 3: Prepare a Kapha-clearing tea — boil 1 inch of fresh ginger, 5 tulsi leaves, 3 black peppercorns, and 1 small cinnamon stick in 2 cups of water for 10 minutes. Strain, add honey, and drink warm twice daily. Step 4: Do steam inhalation with a few drops of eucalyptus oil for 10 minutes before bed to clear congestion. Step 5: Apply a warm compress or a cloth dipped in warm sesame oil to the throat and chest area for 15 minutes.

DIET: Eat warm, light, and easily digestible foods. Warm soups with black pepper and ginger, khichdi, steamed vegetables, and herbal teas are ideal. Drink warm or hot water throughout the day — never cold. Add turmeric, black pepper, ginger, and cinnamon liberally to cooking. Strictly avoid cold milk, ice cream, yogurt, bananas, fried foods, refined sugar, and cold beverages as they all dramatically increase Kapha and mucus production. Avoid eating heavy meals at night.

YOGA: Practice Kapalabhati Pranayama (Skull-Shining Breath). Sit in a comfortable cross-legged position, take a deep breath in, then exhale sharply and forcefully through the nose while pulling the navel toward the spine. The inhalation happens naturally. Do 30 rounds, rest, then repeat 2 more sets. This clears mucus from the respiratory tract, stimulates the lungs, and powerfully reduces Kapha. Follow with Simhasana (Lion Pose) — kneel, open the mouth wide, stick out the tongue, and roar while exhaling to relieve throat tension.

ESCALATE IF: Sore throat is severe and persists for more than 7 days. There is difficulty breathing or swallowing liquids. High fever above 102°F accompanies the sore throat. White patches or pus are visible on the tonsils. There is a persistent cough producing blood-tinged sputum. Throat swelling causes audible stridor (noisy breathing). The sore throat occurs repeatedly (more than 5 times a year), which may indicate chronic tonsillitis requiring medical evaluation.

title: Stomach Pain and Nausea — Udara Shula and Chardi
content:
DISEASE: Udara Shula (Stomach Pain) and Chardi (Nausea/Vomiting). In Ayurveda, stomach pain and nausea indicate a disturbance in the Annavaha Srotas (digestive channels) and a dysfunction of Samana Vayu, the subdosha of Vata responsible for the downward movement of digestion. When this natural downward flow is disrupted, food stagnates, toxins accumulate, and the body may reverse the flow causing nausea and vomiting.

DOSHA: Pitta and Vata doshas are aggravated. Pitta excess inflames the stomach lining, causes excess acid production, and creates a burning quality to the pain. Vata disturbance disrupts the normal peristaltic movement of the gut, causing cramping, spasms, and erratic pain that shifts location. When Samana Vayu moves upward instead of downward (Udavarta), it produces nausea and vomiting.

SYMPTOMS: Sharp or cramping pain in the upper or central abdomen. Nausea that may or may not lead to vomiting. Burning sensation in the stomach area. Loss of appetite or aversion to food. Sour or bitter taste in the mouth. Bloating and heaviness after eating. Restlessness and inability to find a comfortable position. Occasional loose stools or constipation.

ROOT CAUSE: The primary cause is Mandagni (weak digestive fire) combined with Pitta inflammation. Eating incompatible food combinations (viruddha ahara), such as fruit with meals, milk with salty foods, or fish with dairy, confuse the digestive system. Eating when not hungry, emotional eating, consuming stale or reheated food, and eating too quickly without proper chewing all contribute. Stress and anxiety directly aggravate Vata in the gut through the gut-brain connection.

HERBS: 1) Shunti (Zingiber officinale / Dry Ginger) — Take half a teaspoon of dry ginger powder mixed with a pinch of rock salt and a squeeze of lime juice 15 minutes before meals. This kindles agni, prevents nausea, and promotes proper downward movement of digestion. 2) Pudina (Mentha / Mint) — Prepare fresh mint tea by steeping 10-12 fresh mint leaves in hot water for 5 minutes. Drink after meals to soothe the stomach and relieve nausea. 3) Hingvastak Churna — Take half a teaspoon with warm water before meals. This classical formula containing hing (asafoetida), cumin, black pepper, and ginger powerfully corrects digestive disturbances and relieves stomach pain.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Sip on warm water with fresh ginger slices and a few drops of lime juice throughout the day to settle the stomach. Step 2: Prepare ajwain (carom seeds) water by dry-roasting 1 teaspoon of ajwain seeds, crushing them lightly, and boiling in 1 cup of water for 5 minutes. Strain and drink warm. This provides immediate relief from stomach cramps and gas. Step 3: Apply warm castor oil on the navel and abdomen in clockwise circular motions for 10 minutes. Cover with a warm cloth. Step 4: Chew a small piece of fresh ginger with a pinch of rock salt before meals to stimulate agni. Step 5: If nausea is severe, smell fresh cardamom pods or cloves — the aroma helps settle Samana Vayu and reduce the urge to vomit.

DIET: Eat freshly cooked, warm, and simple foods. Plain rice with a little ghee and rock salt, moong dal soup, bottle gourd soup, and steamed rice with buttermilk are ideal during recovery. Avoid raw foods, heavy proteins, fried items, spicy foods, and dairy during acute episodes. Eat small portions every 3-4 hours rather than large meals. Sip warm water or cumin-coriander-fennel tea (equal parts boiled in water) between meals. Resume normal eating gradually once symptoms subside.

YOGA: Practice Apanasana (Knees-to-Chest Pose). Lie on your back, bring both knees to the chest, and wrap your arms around them. Gently rock side to side for 1-2 minutes. This massages the abdominal organs, relieves trapped gas, and restores the downward movement of Apana Vayu. Follow with Supta Baddha Konasana (Reclined Butterfly Pose) — lie back with soles of feet together and knees falling open, hands on the belly, breathing deeply for 5 minutes. This relaxes the abdominal muscles and calms the digestive system.

ESCALATE IF: Stomach pain is sudden, severe, and localized to the right lower abdomen (may indicate appendicitis). There is blood in vomit or stools. Pain is accompanied by high fever and rigidity of the abdominal wall. Persistent vomiting lasting more than 24 hours causing dehydration. Severe pain that radiates to the back (may indicate pancreatitis). Abdominal pain occurs during pregnancy.

title: Back Pain — Kati Shula
content:
DISEASE: Kati Shula (Back Pain) and Gridhrasi (Sciatica). In Ayurveda, the lower back is considered a primary seat of Vata dosha. Back pain is fundamentally a Vata disorder caused by degeneration, dryness, or displacement in the spinal structures. When Vata accumulates in the Asthi dhatu (bone tissue) and Majja dhatu (marrow and nerve tissue), it causes pain, stiffness, and degeneration of the spinal discs and joints.

DOSHA: Vata dosha is primarily aggravated. Vata's dry, cold, light, and mobile qualities cause desiccation of the intervertebral discs, stiffness of the spinal muscles, and irregular nerve impulses. When Vata enters the Asthi dhatu (bone) it causes cracking, popping, and degeneration. When it affects Majja dhatu (nerves), it causes radiating pain along nerve pathways — this is Gridhrasi (sciatica), where pain travels from the lower back down through the buttock and leg.

SYMPTOMS: Dull, aching pain in the lower back that may be constant or intermittent. Stiffness, especially in the morning or after sitting for long periods. Sharp shooting pain radiating down one or both legs (sciatica). Difficulty bending, twisting, or standing up straight. Muscle spasms in the back. Cracking or popping sounds in the spine. Pain worsens in cold weather, with movement, or after physical exertion. Numbness or tingling in the legs in severe cases.

ROOT CAUSE: Vata is aggravated by sedentary lifestyle with prolonged sitting, improper posture, lifting heavy objects incorrectly, lack of adequate nutrition for bone health, excessive travel (especially on rough roads), exposure to cold wind and drafts, suppression of natural urges (especially flatulence and defecation), and emotional stress. The modern lifestyle of sitting in chairs for 8-10 hours creates sustained compression and Vata accumulation in the lumbar spine.

HERBS: 1) Guggulu (Commiphora mukul) — Take Yogaraja Guggulu tablets (2 tablets twice daily with warm water after meals). Guggulu is the premier Ayurvedic herb for musculoskeletal disorders. It penetrates deep into bone and joint tissue, reduces inflammation, and rejuvenates degenerated structures. 2) Bala (Sida cordifolia) — Take 1 teaspoon of Bala powder in warm milk with ghee twice daily. Bala means 'strength' and it directly nourishes muscles, nerves, and connective tissue. 3) Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — Apply Nirgundi oil externally on the affected area. For internal use, take Nirgundi leaf decoction (5 grams boiled in 200 ml water) once daily. It is a powerful natural analgesic and anti-inflammatory.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Perform daily Kati Basti at home — warm sesame oil or Mahanarayan oil in a double boiler to a comfortable temperature. Lie face down and have someone pour the warm oil on your lower back, creating a pool by surrounding the area with a ring of dough made from black gram flour. Let the oil sit for 20-30 minutes. Step 2: Apply a paste of dry ginger powder mixed with warm sesame oil to the lower back and cover with a warm cloth for 30 minutes. Step 3: Take a warm bath with Epsom salt and a few drops of eucalyptus oil to relax the muscles. Step 4: Sleep on a firm surface — avoid very soft mattresses that do not support the spine. Step 5: Practice gentle spinal movements throughout the day — avoid sitting for more than 45 minutes at a stretch.

DIET: Eat warm, unctuous, nourishing foods that pacify Vata. Include ghee liberally — at least 2-3 teaspoons daily in your food. Eat warm soups, stews, cooked root vegetables, whole grains, and healthy fats. Drink warm milk with turmeric and ghee before bed (golden milk). Include sesame seeds, almonds, walnuts, and flaxseeds in your diet. Avoid cold foods, raw vegetables, dry snacks, carbonated beverages, and excessive bitter or astringent foods which increase Vata and worsen dryness in the joints.

YOGA: Practice Marjariasana-Bitilakasana (Cat-Cow Pose). Come onto all fours with wrists under shoulders and knees under hips. On inhale, drop the belly toward the floor, lift the chest and tailbone (Cow). On exhale, round the spine toward the ceiling, tucking the chin and tailbone (Cat). Move slowly between these positions for 2-3 minutes, synchronizing with breath. This gently mobilizes the entire spine, releases tension, and improves circulation to the spinal discs. Follow with Bhujangasana (Cobra Pose) — lie face down, place palms under shoulders, and gently lift the chest while keeping hips on the floor. Hold for 5 breaths. This strengthens the lower back muscles.

ESCALATE IF: Back pain is accompanied by numbness, weakness, or loss of sensation in the legs. There is loss of bowel or bladder control (cauda equina syndrome — emergency). Pain follows a significant fall, injury, or accident. Severe pain does not improve after 4-6 weeks of conservative treatment. Pain is accompanied by unexplained weight loss or fever. There is progressive weakness in the legs that worsens over time.

title: Anxiety and Stress — Chittodvega
content:
DISEASE: Chittodvega (Anxiety) and Mano Avasada (Stress-related Mental Exhaustion). In Ayurveda, mental health disorders are rooted in the imbalance of the three Manogunas — Sattva (clarity), Rajas (agitation), and Tamas (inertia). Anxiety is a Rajasic disturbance amplified by Vata dosha, where the mind becomes excessively active, restless, and unable to find stability. The Prana Vayu (vital air governing the mind) becomes erratic, causing racing thoughts, fear, and a sense of impending doom.

DOSHA: Vata dosha is primarily aggravated, specifically Prana Vayu (governing the mind and senses) and Vyana Vayu (governing circulation and nerve impulses). When Prana Vayu is disturbed, the mind loses its ability to stay present and grounded, jumping between fears about the future and regrets about the past. This creates a cascade of physical symptoms through the nervous system — rapid heartbeat, shallow breathing, sweating, and muscle tension.

SYMPTOMS: Persistent worry and apprehension that feels disproportionate to the situation. Racing thoughts and inability to quiet the mind. Restlessness and inability to sit still. Difficulty falling or staying asleep (insomnia). Rapid heartbeat and palpitations. Shortness of breath or a feeling of tightness in the chest. Trembling or shaking, especially in the hands. Digestive disturbances — loss of appetite, nausea, or irritable bowel. Dry mouth, excessive sweating, and cold hands. Difficulty concentrating and making decisions.

ROOT CAUSE: Vata aggravation through overstimulation of the senses — excessive screen time, constant social media, information overload, and multi-tasking. Irregular daily routines, lack of grounding practices, insufficient sleep, excessive travel, and isolation from nature all destabilize Vata. Suppressed emotions, unresolved trauma, and chronic pressure without adequate support deplete ojas (the substance that provides emotional resilience). Excessive consumption of caffeine, sugar, and processed foods further destabilizes the nervous system.

HERBS: 1) Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Take 1 teaspoon of Ashwagandha powder in warm milk with a pinch of nutmeg before bed. Ashwagandha is classified as a Medhya Rasayana (brain rejuvenative) and is clinically proven to reduce cortisol levels by up to 30%. It calms the nervous system without causing drowsiness. 2) Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — Take 1 teaspoon of Brahmi powder with ghee and honey in the morning. Brahmi directly nourishes the brain, enhances GABA production, and calms Prana Vayu. It is the foremost Ayurvedic herb for anxiety and cognitive function. 3) Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Take 500 mg of Jatamansi powder with warm water before bed. It is nature's tranquilizer, calming the mind without side effects, and is especially effective for anxiety-related insomnia.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Establish a daily Abhyanga (self-oil massage) ritual using warm sesame oil or Brahmi oil. Massage the entire body for 15-20 minutes before your morning shower, paying special attention to the scalp, soles of feet, and behind the ears. This is the single most powerful Vata-calming practice. Step 2: Practice Shirodhara at home — lie down and have someone gently pour a thin stream of warm sesame oil on your forehead (the third eye area) for 15-20 minutes. This profoundly calms the nervous system. Step 3: Create a calming bedtime ritual — dim lights at 8 PM, drink warm Ashwagandha milk, apply Brahmi oil to the soles of your feet, and listen to calming music or chanting. Step 4: Write down your worries in a journal before bed — this externalization technique helps release mental Vata. Step 5: Spend 15-20 minutes in nature daily, preferably walking barefoot on grass or earth (grounding practice).

DIET: Eat warm, grounding, nourishing foods at regular times. The regularity of mealtimes is as important as the food itself for Vata balance. Favor sweet, sour, and salty tastes — warm oatmeal with ghee, sweet potatoes, warm milk, almonds, dates, and bananas. Include healthy fats generously — ghee, coconut oil, avocado, and nuts. Avoid stimulants entirely — caffeine, alcohol, refined sugar, and energy drinks directly destabilize the nervous system. Avoid light, dry, cold, and raw foods which increase Vata. Eat dinner early (by 7 PM) and keep it warm and easy to digest.

YOGA: Practice Nadi Shodhana Pranayama (Alternate Nostril Breathing). Sit comfortably with spine erect. Close the right nostril with the right thumb and inhale through the left nostril for a count of 4. Close both nostrils and hold for a count of 4. Release the right nostril and exhale for a count of 8. Inhale through the right nostril for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale through the left for 8. This is one round. Practice 10-15 rounds. This pranayama directly balances Prana Vayu, harmonizes the left and right hemispheres of the brain, activates the parasympathetic nervous system, and produces a profound sense of calm within minutes.

ESCALATE IF: Anxiety is accompanied by thoughts of self-harm or suicide — seek help immediately. Panic attacks are frequent (more than once a week) and debilitating. Anxiety prevents you from performing daily activities, going to work, or maintaining relationships. There is substance use (alcohol or drugs) to cope with anxiety. Physical symptoms like chest pain or difficulty breathing are severe enough to mimic a heart attack. Anxiety follows a traumatic event and includes flashbacks, nightmares, or severe avoidance behaviors.

title: Skin Irritation and Rashes — Twak Vikara
content:
DISEASE: Twak Vikara (Skin Disorders). In Ayurveda, the skin is a reflection of the health of Rakta Dhatu (blood tissue) and Bhrajaka Pitta (the subdosha of Pitta residing in the skin). When Pitta dosha is aggravated, it heats the blood, which then manifests externally as redness, rashes, burning, itching, and inflammation of the skin. Ayurveda considers most skin disorders to be blood-related (Raktaja) conditions.

DOSHA: Pitta dosha is primarily aggravated, specifically Bhrajaka Pitta (skin Pitta) and Ranjaka Pitta (liver Pitta). The liver plays a crucial role — when Ranjaka Pitta is excessive, the liver cannot properly purify the blood, and toxins (ama) accumulate in the Rakta Dhatu. These toxins then find their way to the skin, the largest organ of elimination, causing eruptions, rashes, and irritation. Heat, inflammation, and redness are hallmarks of Pitta skin conditions.

SYMPTOMS: Red, inflamed patches on the skin that may be warm to touch. Itching that worsens with heat, hot water, or sun exposure. Burning or stinging sensation on the affected areas. Hives or raised welts that appear suddenly. Dry, flaky patches that may crack and weep. Discoloration of the skin — redness or darkening. Rashes that worsen with spicy food, alcohol, or stress. Sensitivity to synthetic fabrics, chemicals, or certain cosmetics.

ROOT CAUSE: Pitta aggravation through excess intake of hot, spicy, sour, salty, and fermented foods. Alcohol, red meat, excessive sun exposure, chemical-laden skincare products, and synthetic clothing all aggravate Bhrajaka Pitta. Emotional factors — suppressed anger, frustration, and intense competitiveness — heat the blood from within. Impaired liver function (from processed foods, alcohol, or medications) reduces the blood's purification capacity, pushing toxins to the skin for elimination.

HERBS: 1) Neem (Azadirachta indica) — Take 1 teaspoon of neem leaf powder with warm water on an empty stomach, or drink neem leaf tea (5-6 fresh leaves steeped in hot water). Externally, apply neem oil diluted with coconut oil to affected areas. Neem is the most powerful blood purifier in Ayurveda and has strong antibacterial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory properties. 2) Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Take 1 teaspoon of Manjistha powder with warm water twice daily. Manjistha is the premier Ayurvedic herb for blood purification and skin health. It cools the blood, reduces inflammation, and improves skin complexion. 3) Khadira (Acacia catechu) — Take Khadirarishta (a classical fermented preparation) 15-20 ml with equal water after meals twice daily. Khadira is specifically indicated for chronic skin conditions and deeply purifies the blood.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Apply fresh aloe vera gel directly to the affected skin areas 2-3 times daily. Aloe vera is cooling, soothing, and promotes skin healing. Step 2: Prepare a cooling skin paste by mixing sandalwood powder, rose water, and a pinch of turmeric into a smooth paste. Apply to irritated areas and leave for 20 minutes before washing with cool water. Step 3: Take a lukewarm bath (never hot) with a handful of neem leaves and 2 tablespoons of colloidal oatmeal. Soak for 15 minutes. Step 4: Drink a blood-purifying tea daily — boil 1 teaspoon each of coriander seeds, fennel seeds, and cumin seeds in 3 cups of water for 5 minutes. Strain and drink warm throughout the day. Step 5: Apply coconut oil infused with neem leaves (heat coconut oil with neem leaves on low for 15 minutes, strain, and cool) to the skin before bed.

DIET: Eat cooling, bitter, and astringent foods that pacify Pitta and purify the blood. Bitter gourd (karela), neem flowers, turmeric, leafy greens, cucumber, watermelon, pomegranate, and coconut are excellent. Drink coconut water, aloe vera juice, and coriander water daily. Strictly avoid hot, spicy, sour, and fermented foods — chilies, tomatoes, citrus, vinegar, pickles, alcohol, and red meat. Avoid nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, eggplant, bell peppers) as they can aggravate skin conditions. Minimize processed foods, refined sugar, and dairy products.

YOGA: Practice Sheetali Pranayama (Cooling Breath). Sit comfortably, curl the tongue into a tube shape, inhale slowly and deeply through the curled tongue (you will feel the cool air), close the mouth, and exhale slowly through both nostrils. If you cannot curl the tongue, practice Sheetkari Pranayama — clench the teeth lightly, part the lips, and inhale through the teeth with a hissing sound. Practice 15-20 rounds. This cools the entire body from within, directly reduces Pitta in the blood, and calms the inflammatory response that drives skin eruptions.

ESCALATE IF: Skin rash is rapidly spreading or covering large areas of the body. There is significant swelling, especially of the face, lips, or throat (allergic reaction — emergency). Skin lesions are oozing pus or show signs of bacterial infection. Rash is accompanied by high fever and joint pain. There are blisters that are painful and spreading. Skin condition does not improve after 3-4 weeks of home treatment. There is a new mole or existing mole that has changed in size, shape, or color.

title: Dizziness and Vertigo — Bhrama
content:
DISEASE: Bhrama (Dizziness and Vertigo). In Ayurveda, Bhrama is described as a condition where the person perceives the world as spinning or moving when it is still. It is classified under disorders of the Shirah (head) and is closely linked to disturbances in Prana Vayu (the vital air governing the brain and senses) and the Rakta Dhatu (blood tissue). It can range from mild lightheadedness to severe vertigo with nausea and loss of balance.

DOSHA: Vata and Pitta doshas are aggravated. Vata disturbance — specifically Prana Vayu — disrupts the sensory processing in the brain, causing disorientation and unsteadiness. Pitta aggravation heats the blood and Rakta Dhatu, causing increased blood pressure and disturbed circulation to the brain. When both are aggravated together, the result is a spinning sensation (vertigo) combined with visual disturbances, nausea, and difficulty maintaining balance.

SYMPTOMS: Sensation of the room spinning around you (vertigo). Lightheadedness, especially when standing up quickly. Unsteadiness and difficulty walking straight. Nausea and vomiting accompanying the spinning sensation. Ringing in the ears (tinnitus). Blurred vision or difficulty focusing. Sweating and pallor during episodes. Episodes triggered by sudden head movements, bending down, or looking up. Feeling of floating or being disconnected from the body.

ROOT CAUSE: Ayurveda identifies multiple root causes. Vata aggravation through fasting, dehydration, sleep deprivation, excessive physical or mental exertion, and standing for prolonged periods reduces blood flow to the brain. Pitta aggravation through anger, sun exposure, and overheating causes blood pressure fluctuations. Weakened Rasa Dhatu (plasma) from poor nutrition, anemia, or chronic illness means insufficient nourishment reaches the brain. Ama (toxins) blocking the channels to the head prevent proper circulation. Cervical spondylosis (Greeva Stambha) can also cause positional vertigo by compressing blood vessels in the neck.

HERBS: 1) Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — Take 1 teaspoon of Shankhapushpi powder with warm milk twice daily. This herb directly nourishes the brain, stabilizes Prana Vayu, improves cerebral circulation, and is one of the four Medhya Rasayana (brain tonics) described in Ayurveda. 2) Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus / Indian Sarsaparilla) — Take 1 teaspoon of Sariva powder with cool water twice daily. It cools the blood, reduces Pitta-related dizziness, and purifies the circulatory channels. 3) Amalaki (Emblica officinalis) — Take 1 teaspoon of Amalaki powder with water in the morning. Rich in Vitamin C and antioxidants, Amalaki strengthens the Rasa and Rakta Dhatus, improves iron absorption, and addresses anemia-related dizziness.

HOME REMEDY: Step 1: Immediately when dizziness strikes, sit or lie down in a safe place. Close your eyes and take 10 slow, deep breaths through the nose. Place a hand on the abdomen and feel it rise and fall — this grounds Prana Vayu and often stops the spinning. Step 2: Drink a glass of warm water with a teaspoon of honey, a pinch of salt, and a squeeze of lemon. This quickly corrects mild dehydration and low blood sugar, two common triggers. Step 3: Practice the Epley-inspired Ayurvedic head positioning — lie on the side opposite to the direction of spinning, stay for 2 minutes, then slowly roll to the other side and stay for 2 minutes. This helps if the cause is positional vertigo. Step 4: Gently massage the Adhipati Marma point (the crown of the head) and the Shankha Marma points (the temples) with Brahmi oil in small circular motions for 5 minutes. Step 5: Soak 5-6 almonds and 2-3 pumpkin seeds overnight. Grind into a paste in the morning, mix with warm milk and a pinch of cardamom. Drink daily as a brain-nourishing tonic.

DIET: Eat regular, nourishing meals — never skip meals as fasting aggravates both Vata and low blood sugar that trigger dizziness. Include iron-rich foods — pomegranate, beetroot, spinach, dates, jaggery, and black sesame seeds to address anemia. Eat foods rich in healthy fats — ghee, almonds, walnuts, avocado — to nourish the brain. Stay well hydrated — drink at least 8 glasses of warm water daily, more in hot weather. Avoid sudden position changes after meals. Avoid excessive salt (raises blood pressure), caffeine (dehydrates), and alcohol (directly causes vertigo). Include soaked almonds, raisins, and milk as a daily snack for sustained brain nourishment.

YOGA: Practice Bhramari Pranayama (Humming Bee Breath). Sit comfortably, close the eyes, and place the index fingers gently on the tragus cartilage of the ears to partially block external sound. Take a deep breath in and while exhaling, make a steady humming sound like a bee. Feel the vibration in the head and brain. Practice 7-10 rounds. The vibrations from Bhramari stimulate the vestibular system, calm Prana Vayu, reduce tinnitus, and restore balance. This pranayama also reduces blood pressure and calms Pitta, addressing two common causes of dizziness. Follow with Tadasana (Mountain Pose) — stand with feet hip-width apart, ground through the feet, and focus on a fixed point to improve balance.

ESCALATE IF: Dizziness is sudden, severe, and accompanied by slurred speech, facial drooping, or arm weakness (stroke symptoms — emergency). Vertigo is accompanied by sudden hearing loss in one or both ears. There is persistent vomiting that prevents keeping any fluids down. Dizziness follows a head injury. Episodes are becoming more frequent or more severe over time. Dizziness is accompanied by high fever and severe headache. There is fainting or loss of consciousness during dizzy episodes.
